title: Motor Oil on Cotton
timeEstimate: 20–45 minutes; aged or heavy stains need multiple cycles
stainChemistry: Motor oil is much heavier and more tenacious than cooking oil — petroleum hydrocarbons (C15–C50 alkanes + cycloalkanes + aromatics) plus engine additives (zinc dialkyldithiophosphate anti-wear, dispersants, detergents, viscosity modifiers). Used motor oil adds carbon black particulate from combustion. The heavy-molecular-weight hydrocarbons bond to cotton through deep capillary penetration and hydrophobic affinity. Water does nothing; the oil must be dissolved by a matching petroleum solvent, then emulsified by surfactant, then rinsed. Cotton tolerates POG, mineral spirits, IPA, NSD, and hot post-chemistry wash at room temperature.
whyThisWorks: Heavy petroleum needs heavy petroleum solvent. POG or mineral spirits dissolves the oil + additives. Cornstarch or cornmeal powder absorbs the bulk first (pro-grade tip that works at home too) — reduces the mass the solvent has to handle. Once the solvent has mobilized the oil, NSD emulsifies it so water can rinse it out. Hot wash finishes. The carbon black particulate from used motor oil is what often leaves a gray shadow after the oil is gone — that's a separate mechanical-removal problem and sometimes doesn't fully clear.
defaultAssumption: Used motor oil (black, with combustion particulate) vs fresh motor oil (amber, just the hydrocarbons) behave differently — used is significantly harder because of the carbon. Assume used unless the user says otherwise. Mechanic shop stains on work clothes are often heavily saturated — multiple cycles expected.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Cover the stain completely with cornstarch, cornmeal, or fuller's earth. A visibly thick layer, not a dusting. Let it sit 20 minutes minimum — longer on heavier stains. The powder wicks oil out of the fiber without any moisture. Brush off with a soft brush. Repeat if the stain is still visibly wet-looking. This step pulls 40–60% of the oil out before any solvent touches the fabric.

spottingProtocol.technique: Solvent on cloth, tamp from back
spottingProtocol.equipment: POG or mineral spirits on white cloth, catch cloth under stain
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply POG or mineral spirits to a clean white cloth. Tamp the stain from the BACK of the fabric, with a catch cloth UNDER. The solvent dissolves the hydrocarbons + additives; the oil transfers out into the under-cloth and the pressing cloth. Flip cloth frequently. Ventilate — mineral spirits have flammable fumes. 3–5 passes on heavy stains.
Apply NSD, work with a soft bristle brush. NSD emulsifies the now-dispersed oil so water can carry it out. Rinse cool from the back.
Used motor oil often needs 2–3 full solvent + NSD cycles. Each pass lifts another layer. Stop when the stain stops visibly lightening between passes.

Launder hot per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. A gray shadow may remain if there's carbon black particulate — that's mechanical, not chemical, and sometimes doesn't fully clear. Set expectations honestly.

steps: 1. Absorbent powder preload (cornstarch / cornmeal / fuller's earth). 20+ min. Brush off.
2. POG or mineral spirits on a white cloth, tamp from back with catch cloth under. 3–5 passes.
3. NSD to emulsify — 5–10 min with brush. Cool rinse.
4. Repeat solvent + NSD cycle as needed.
professionalProtocol.temperature: room temp through spotting; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: Ventilate — mineral spirits and POG have flammable fumes
Catch cloth UNDER + pressing cloth on TOP — prevents sideways oil spread
Heavy used-oil stains need multiple cycles — budget accordingly
Carbon black particulate from used oil may leave a gray shadow that doesn't fully clear — set expectations

homeSolutions.agent: Cornstarch or Baby Powder First — Pull Oil Out
homeSolutions.instruction: Before any solvent touches the fabric, cover the oil stain completely with cornstarch, baking soda, or plain talc baby powder. A thick layer — you shouldn't see the stain through it. Let it sit 30 minutes to an hour. The powder wicks the oil UP out of the cotton fibers without introducing any moisture. Brush off with a soft brush. You'll see the stain dramatically smaller already.

homeSolutions.agent: Place a White Towel UNDER the Stain
homeSolutions.instruction: Fold a clean white towel and place it UNDER the stained area. When the solvent mobilizes the oil in the next step, it needs somewhere to go. The catch cloth pulls it downward.

homeSolutions.agent: Paint Thinner (Mineral Spirits) on a White Rag
homeSolutions.instruction: Apply mineral spirits (paint thinner — any hardware store) to a clean white rag. Press the rag onto the stain from the BACK of the fabric, over the catch cloth. Flip the rag to a clean section every few seconds. The oil transfers out. Ventilate the area — open a window, no flames, no smoking. Expect 3–5 rounds on heavy motor oil.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Mineral spirits are flammable. Ventilate. Keep away from any ignition source. If you don't have mineral spirits, rubbing alcohol is a weaker alternative — takes more passes.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn + Warm Water — Emulsify What's Left
homeSolutions.instruction: Work a generous amount of Dawn dish soap directly into the spot with a soft brush or fingertip. Dawn is formulated to break up petroleum oils (it's what wildlife rehabilitators use to clean oil-spill birds). Let it sit 5–10 minutes, rinse cool, repeat. This step often lifts more color than the solvent pass because Dawn carries out the emulsified oil.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash — Multiple Cycles May Be Needed
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Use a heavy-duty or "grease-cutting" detergent if you have one (Tide Heavy Duty, Persil ProClean). For work shirts heavily saturated in motor oil, run TWO full wash cycles before drying — inspect between. Inspect BEFORE the dryer — any remaining oil cures into the fiber under heat.

Used motor oil contains carbon black from combustion. Even when the oil itself is fully gone, a gray shadow sometimes remains — that's carbon particulate stuck in the fibers, and home chemistry doesn't always clear it. Don't keep cycling if the stain has plateaued. Take it to a dry cleaner; they have hot-water extraction and specialized solvents. Or, for work clothes that see motor oil regularly, accept that the fabric may carry a permanent shadow — a badge of honest work, not a failure.

homeSolutions.agent: Escalate for Fresh Stains on Non-Work Clothes
homeSolutions.instruction: If this is a FRESH motor oil stain on a nice garment (not a mechanic's work shirt), and two home rounds haven't cleared it, don't keep cycling — get it to a dry cleaner fast, while the oil is still recent. Tell them: "Motor oil on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], used [type of oil if known], home-treated with cornstarch + mineral spirits + Dawn."

steps: 1. Cornstarch / baking soda / baby powder preload, 30–60 min, brush off.
3. Mineral spirits on a white rag, tamp from BACK. Flip cloth. 3–5 passes. Ventilate.
4. Dawn + soft brush, generous amount, 5–10 min dwell, rinse. Repeat.
5. Hot wash per care label — heavy-duty detergent if available. Double cycle on heavy stains.
7. Accept plateau: used motor oil's carbon may leave a permanent gray shadow.
products: Clean white rags or towels (catch + pressing)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if two home rounds haven't cleared the stain, if the dryer has already baked it in, or if the plateau is carbon-black shadow from used oil (home chemistry doesn't always clear that).
warnings: Catch cloth + pressing cloth — sideways oil spread is easy without them
Inspect BEFORE the dryer — heat sets residual oil permanently
Used motor oil's carbon may leave a permanent gray shadow — set honest expectations
Never near open flame or ignition source

neverDo: Never use solvent near open flame — mineral spirits are flammable
Never skip the powder preload on heavy motor oil — it cuts the work in half
Never pour solvent directly on fabric — tamp with cloth, catch cloth underneath
Never hot-dry residual motor oil — locks the stain in permanently
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates POG, mineral spirits, IPA, NSD, hot wash
Dyed cotton: mineral spirits usually safe; inside-seam-test if concerned
Heat-durable fiber once oil is cleared

materialWarnings: Powder preload cuts the work in half
Carbon black from used oil may leave a permanent shadow

escalation.when: Motor oil has been hot-dried, two home rounds didn't clear it, or the residual gray shadow is from carbon black and won't fade.
escalation.whatToTell: "Motor oil on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / older], [fresh oil / used oil with combustion carbon], home-treated with cornstarch + mineral spirits + Dawn + heavy-duty hot wash. [Hot-washed / not yet laundered.]"

contextQuestions: Fresh motor oil (amber, unused) or used oil (black, with combustion residue)?
Has it been through a hot wash or dryer?
Is this a mechanic's work shirt (saturation common) or a one-off accident?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Used motor oil vs fresh motor oil is the main variable — used has carbon black that chemistry can't fully remove. Honest plateau-acknowledgment is part of the protocol.
